ai_strategy.summary: KU operates a system-wide AI strategy articulated through the cross-campus KU AI Taskforce (Lawrence + KUMC), which sets responsible-AI principles, approves approved-tool lists, and coordinates academic, research, and administrative guidance. The strategy centers on seven principles — Human Centeredness, Value Alignment, Equitability, Privacy/Security/Safety, Accountability, Transparency, and Validity — and aims to position KU as a national leader in ethical AI. Strategy execution leans on Microsoft Copilot as the default enterprise tool, FLITE and the Enhanced AI Center as research engines, and the Center for Teaching Excellence as the faculty-development arm.

key_provisions: Seven responsible-AI principles govern campus use
Non-public data must only be entered into approved enterprise tools (Microsoft Copilot with enterprise data protection, Adobe Firefly, Databricks at KUMC)
FERPA, HIPAA, and GDPR compliance mandatory
No specific university-wide syllabus policy — faculty set course-level AI rules
GenAI plagiarism detectors discouraged due to bias and false positives
Users remain accountable for GenAI outputs (hallucinations)
AI use in research must be disclosed and comply with funder requirements
Copilot may not be used with Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI)
ai_policy.summary: KU has a comprehensive, principle-based acceptable-use framework rather than a prescriptive syllabus mandate. Use of generative AI is permitted across faculty, students, staff, and research, but tools must be approved for any non-public data, and faculty set discipline-specific course policies. The framework explicitly addresses FERPA/HIPAA/GDPR compliance and flags bias, privacy, and accountability as primary risks.

bodies.name: KU AI Taskforce
bodies.scope: Cross-campus (KU Lawrence + KUMC) strategic guidance, oversight, and policy for AI in academics, administration, and research; meets biweekly; two co-chairs, 17+ members spanning IT, Legal/Compliance, Libraries, KUMC, Research, Faculty Senate, Marketing, and Institutional Research.

bodies.name: Center for Teaching Excellence — KUCTE AI
bodies.scope: Faculty-facing AI-in-teaching guidance, syllabus templates, podcast series, and AI literacy professional development.

bodies.name: Enhanced Artificial Intelligence Center for Human-Focused Research (KUMC)
bodies.scope: KUMC-side governance of AI research infrastructure (Databricks, Azure, MLFlow) and HIPAA-compliant AI applications in health/education.

governance_structure.summary: KU's AI governance runs through a formal Taskforce co-chaired across KU Lawrence and the Medical Center, supported by subject-matter offices at the Center for Teaching Excellence (faculty development) and the Enhanced AI Center (research infrastructure). The Taskforce integrates the CIO, CISO, Chief Strategy & Data Officer, privacy officer, compliance officer, and faculty representatives, giving it unusually broad standing authority for an R1.

initiatives.title: KU AI Taskforce and university AI guidelines
initiatives.description: KU established a cross-campus AI Taskforce (Lawrence + KUMC) that meets biweekly, with two co-chairs and 17+ members from IT, compliance, legal, libraries, research, and faculty. The taskforce published seven guiding principles (human centeredness, value alignment, equitability, privacy, accountability, transparency, validity) and a public AI guidelines site (ai.ku.edu).
initiatives.significance: An R1 adopting a standing, cross-campus governance body with published principles signals a shift from ad-hoc statements to institutional AI stewardship.

investment.funding_source: Institutional — Office of the Provost / Chancellor
investment.description: Standing taskforce with diverse membership; budget not publicly disclosed.

collaborations.description: Dual-campus coordination with co-chairs from each.

initiatives.policy_implications: Creates an enduring governance venue to keep policy aligned with state (Kansas HB2313, HB2592), federal, and accreditor expectations.

initiatives.title: Microsoft 365 Copilot campus-wide rollout
initiatives.description: KU has designated Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat as the approved enterprise GenAI tool for all students, faculty, staff, and researchers on the Lawrence campus. Copilot Chat with enterprise data protection is already available; Microsoft 365 Copilot Full License is rolling out winter 2025 at a projected ~$220/user/year, and Copilot Studio supports custom agents.
initiatives.significance: Represents one of the clearer public examples of an R1 selecting a single default enterprise LLM and exposing it to the whole campus while explicitly restricting higher-sensitivity data.

investment.description: Projected ~$220 per full-license user per year plus Copilot Studio pay-as-you-go; base Copilot Chat included in M365 license.

collaborations.description: Enterprise license with data-protection terms; no training of Microsoft models on KU data.

tools_and_vendors.name: Microsoft 365 Copilot Chat
tools_and_vendors.role: Default campus GenAI chat assistant with enterprise data protection

tools_and_vendors.name: Microsoft 365 Copilot Full License
tools_and_vendors.role: Embedded Copilot across Word/Excel/PowerPoint/Outlook for approved users

initiatives.policy_implications: Standardizing on one vendor reduces shadow-IT risk and gives the CISO a single place to enforce data classification boundaries, but concentrates vendor dependence on Microsoft.

initiatives.title: Adobe Firefly and Adobe Express generative AI access
initiatives.description: KU makes Adobe Firefly (generative image AI) and Adobe Express available to all Lawrence students, faculty, staff, and researchers as approved creative-AI tools under the institutional Adobe license.
initiatives.significance: Extends universal GenAI access beyond text to image generation, improving tool equity for creative and visual disciplines.

investment.description: Bundled in KU's Adobe institutional subscription.

collaborations.description: Institution-wide licensing covering Creative Cloud, Express, and Firefly.

tools_and_vendors.name: Adobe Express
tools_and_vendors.role: Creative suite with generative fill and text-to-image

initiatives.policy_implications: Pairs with Copilot to give a complete multimodal default-stack; sidesteps equity concerns from paid-only creative AI tools.

initiatives.title: Falling Into AI campuswide summit with Google
initiatives.description: KU partnered with Google to host 'Falling Into AI,' its first campus-wide AI week (Sept 29 – Oct 3, 2025), featuring Google Cloud keynotes, research showcases from FLITE, CTE, CPPR, and I2S, and sessions on AI careers, research collaboration, and healthcare applications.
initiatives.significance: One of the first R1 'AI weeks' explicitly co-produced with Google for Education; Google publicly affirmed KU retained IP rights over innovations developed during the event.

investment.description: In-kind Google speaker support plus KU programming.

collaborations.description: Google Cloud education team delivered keynote and breakout sessions across the week.

tools_and_vendors.name: Google Gemini
tools_and_vendors.role: Referenced in demonstrations and discussion (not the campus default)

initiatives.policy_implications: Signals KU is willing to engage multiple vendor ecosystems for learning even while standardizing on Microsoft for enterprise use.

initiatives.title: Flexible Learning through Innovations in Technology & Education (FLITE) center
initiatives.description: Announced in September 2023 under the Achievement & Assessment Institute and led by Williamson Family Distinguished Professor Lisa Dieker, FLITE is a center for AI, XR, and emerging technologies aimed at students with disabilities and teacher preparation. It houses AI tutoring agents, VR/AI social-skills tools, and the AI-SCORE writing platform.
initiatives.significance: FLITE is emerging as the university's flagship AI-for-education center and its principal anchor for equity and disability-focused AI research.

investment.funding_source: Multiple federal (OSEP) grants; AAI institutional support
investment.description: Individual projects inside FLITE include two OSEP grants of $5M+ each (Maggie Mosher, PI); totals at least $10M across active awards.

collaborations.description: Cross-university research on AI for students with disabilities.

partners: U.S. Department of Education — Office of Special Education Programs (OSEP)

tools_and_vendors.name: AI-SCORE
tools_and_vendors.role: AI-assisted writing competency tool for students with learning differences

initiatives.title: Enhanced AI Center for Human-Focused Research (KUMC)
initiatives.description: The Enhanced Artificial Intelligence Center at the KU Medical Center provides a HIPAA-compliant Digital Research Platform (Azure + Databricks + Delta Lake + MLFlow) that supports AI model training on clinical data, biomedical discovery, and public-health analytics across medicine, nursing, public health, engineering, and informatics.
initiatives.significance: Combines governance, secure compute, and ML lifecycle tooling in a single medical-research platform — a template many R1 AMCs are aiming for.

investment.description: Azure-based compute with on-demand GPU scaling and Databricks data lakehouse; exact recurring cost not disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: Shows how an R1 academic medical center can align AI research enablement with HIPAA-grade data governance.

initiatives.title: Reliable Intelligence and Medical Innovation Lab (RIMI Lab)
initiatives.description: Led by Dr. Zhiguo Zhou in KUMC's Department of Biostatistics and Data Science, RIMI Lab develops reliable-AI theory (balance, safety, robustness, interpretability) and applies it to cancer imaging (lung cancer screening, triple-negative breast cancer, immunotherapy response) and Alzheimer's disease pathology.
initiatives.significance: RIMI is one of the few academic labs publicly framing research around 'reliable AI' as its core theoretical program rather than a task-specific application area.

investment.description: Specific funding totals not disclosed publicly.

initiatives.title: Center for Cyber-Social Dynamics (CCSD) — AI ethics and fairness research
initiatives.description: Housed in the Institute for Information Sciences and directed by Philosophy professor John Symons, CCSD studies AI ethics, fairness in ML, human-AI interaction, loneliness and AI, privacy, bias, and the politics of knowledge. Received two awards in early 2025 for research on human-AI interaction and LLM vulnerabilities.
initiatives.significance: Provides a humanities-and-philosophy counterweight to technical AI centers, explicitly framing research around epistemic injustice and ethical trust.

investment.description: Two 2025 research awards focused on human-AI interaction and LLM vulnerabilities; exact totals not disclosed.

collaborations.description: Interdisciplinary collaborations across philosophy, CS, law, and social sciences.

initiatives.policy_implications: Generates scholarship that directly informs KU's AI ethics posture and national debates on algorithmic fairness.

initiatives.title: AI Bill of Rights in Education — Kathryn Conrad
initiatives.description: KU English professor Kathryn Conrad authored 'A Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights for Education' in the journal Critical AI (2024), articulating rights for educators and students including critical AI literacy, participation in tool procurement, protection of student privacy/creative control, and appeal structures for AI-driven decisions.
initiatives.significance: A KU-authored framework that has been cited in national higher-ed AI policy discourse and shapes KU's own ethics posture.

investment.description: Peer-reviewed publication in Critical AI (Duke University Press).

collaborations.description: Co-direction of the AI & Digital Literacy project.

initiatives.policy_implications: Establishes student- and educator-centric rights language that informs KU's seven guiding principles.

initiatives.title: CIDDL — National Center for AI in Special Education (Ed Tech)
initiatives.description: In February 2026, KU (James Basham, PI; Institute for Information Sciences) and the University of Central Florida received a five-year, $3.5M U.S. Department of Education OSEP grant to continue and expand the Center for Innovation, Design & Digital Learning as the National Center to Improve Faculty Capacity to Use Educational Technology in Special Education Personnel and Leadership Preparation Programs.
initiatives.significance: National-scope federal funding reinforces KU's leadership niche in AI for disability and special-education faculty development.

investment.funding_source: U.S. Department of Education — Office of Special Education Programs

partners: University of Central Florida — Toni Jennings Exceptional Education Institute

initiatives.policy_implications: Reinforces a federal pipeline of AI-for-accessibility resources into teacher-prep programs nationally.

initiatives.title: AI and Digital Literacy Institute (AIDL 2025) — NEH-funded
initiatives.description: Hosted by the Hall Center for the Humanities (June 2–6, 2025), the AIDL Institute trained secondary, community-college, and college humanities educators in critical AI literacy. Funded by NEH, the Reiss-Clark Fund, the Gunn Center for Science Fiction, the KU Department of English, and the William T. Kemper Foundation; partnered with the National Humanities Center.
initiatives.significance: Applies humanities-led critical AI literacy as a sector-wide faculty-development model rather than a technologist-only training.

investment.funding_source: National Endowment for the Humanities + donor funds
investment.description: NEH Institutes for Advanced Topics in the Digital Humanities award of $218,732 plus additional KU donor support.

initiatives.policy_implications: Places a humanities frame — ethics, equity, labor, copyright — at the center of AI faculty development.

initiatives.title: Center for Teaching Excellence — KUCTE AI program
initiatives.description: KU's Center for Teaching Excellence maintains a dedicated KUCTE AI resource hub covering syllabus policy templates, AI-and-assessment guidance, faculty tutorials on Copilot/ChatGPT/Claude/Gemini, podcasts with faculty fellows, and an academic-integrity framework that explicitly discourages biased AI plagiarism detectors.
initiatives.significance: A durable, well-resourced faculty-development program reduces the learning curve for instructors adopting AI at scale.

investment.description: Ongoing institutional support for CTE AI programming.

initiatives.policy_implications: Operationalizes 'faculty discretion' by giving instructors concrete tools and templates rather than a blanket university rule.

initiatives.title: Graduate Certificate in Data Science (EECS) and undergraduate Data Science certificate
initiatives.description: KU's School of Engineering offers a Graduate Certificate in Data Science (4 courses / 11+ credit hours) and an online Applied Data Science graduate certificate (15 credit hours, 5 courses). The Psychology department launched an undergraduate Data Science certificate in Fall 2022; enrollment grew from 12 to 52 students by Fall 2024.
initiatives.significance: Steady growth of data-science credentials gives KU a foundation to launch dedicated AI programs; demand is visible in the 4x undergrad enrollment increase.

investment.description: Tuition-funded credential programs housed in EECS and Psychology.

initiatives.title: BigJay HPC cluster and KU Community Cluster (NSF MRI)
initiatives.description: KU's Center for Research Computing operates BigJay — a mixed CPU/high-memory CPU/GPU cluster acquired with NSF MRI funding (award 2117449). Online since August 2022, BigJay provides ~42% of KU's HPC capacity, averaging 85% overall and 83% GPU utilization. Housed in the Advanced Computing Facility alongside ITTC servers.
initiatives.significance: Existing GPU capacity gives KU a local alternative to cloud-only AI research and anchors the Lawrence-side AI/ML workloads.

investment.description: NSF MRI funded acquisition of HPC cluster; exact award amount not stated in public pages referenced.

tools_and_vendors.name: BigJay
tools_and_vendors.role: Mixed CPU/GPU HPC cluster for research workloads

initiatives.title: KU Innovation Park AI-for-entrepreneurs programming
initiatives.description: KU Innovation Park — the largest business incubator in Kansas with a satellite at KUMC — runs educational sessions for AI-using startups (e.g., 'AI & IP: what entrepreneurs need to know') and supports commercialization via the Oread Angel Investors network and the KU Innovation Park Accelerator.
initiatives.significance: Connects the campus AI research base to regional economic development and startup formation.

investment.funding_source: KU Innovation Park, Oread Angel Investors, KU Office of Research
investment.description: Ongoing incubator, accelerator, and angel-investment programming.

initiatives.title: Kansas state AI platform restrictions (HB 2313)
initiatives.description: Kansas enacted legislation in 2025 (Senate Substitute for HB 2313) prohibiting state-owned or issued devices from accessing 'AI platforms of concern' and requiring state agency networks to block such access, directly affecting KU's enterprise tool selection and network posture.
initiatives.significance: A concrete example of how state-level AI-restriction legislation is shaping R1 procurement and network controls.

investment.description: Compliance costs absorbed by IT/CISO operations.

initiatives.policy_implications: Constrains which foreign or unvetted AI platforms can be used on state-funded devices; reinforces KU's Microsoft-first default.

EECS Data Science and Engineering pathway

computing_infrastructure.summary: KU operates BigJay, a mixed CPU/high-memory CPU/GPU cluster funded by an NSF MRI award (NSF 2117449), online since August 2022 and providing roughly 42% of KU's HPC capacity (85% average, 83% GPU utilization). The Advanced Computing Facility also houses ITTC servers. On the KUMC side, the Enhanced AI Center provides a Databricks + Azure GPU environment with MLFlow for HIPAA-compliant ML research.

equity_and_access_initiatives.description: All KU students/faculty/staff have institutional access to Copilot Chat with enterprise data protection, explicitly so that free-tier inequities don't disadvantage students who can't pay for premium tools.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: FLITE AI-for-disability research portfolio
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: FLITE and the Life Span Institute develop AI/XR tools that specifically center students with disabilities, including AI-SCORE and VOISS/iKNOW.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: KUCTE discouragement of biased AI detection tools
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: CTE explicitly warns against AI plagiarism detectors because they introduce bias against non-native English speakers and students with disabilities.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: CCSD fairness-in-ML research
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Center for Cyber-Social Dynamics runs programming on algorithmic fairness, epistemic injustice, and bias in machine learning.

regulatory_context.summary: Kansas enacted Senate Substitute for HB 2313 (2025) prohibiting state-owned/issued devices and agency networks from accessing 'AI platforms of concern,' which applies to KU as a state university. Kansas also considered HB 2592 to create an AI advisory task force spanning the legislature, executive branch, attorney general, higher education, and the private sector. The Kansas Board of Regents (KU's governing body) does not publish a system-wide AI policy beyond general academic-program oversight; each Regents institution sets its own AI framework.

narrative_summary: The University of Kansas has built one of the more mature AI postures among public R1s in the Midwest, organized around a cross-campus AI Taskforce that links KU Lawrence and the KU Medical Center under a single governance umbrella. The taskforce is unusually broad — spanning the CIO, CISO, Chief Strategy & Data Officer, privacy officer, compliance officer, Libraries, Faculty Senate, Marketing, and KUMC informatics — and publishes a principles-based framework grounded in human centeredness, equitability, privacy, accountability, transparency, and validity. KU has not adopted a blanket classroom policy; instead, faculty discretion is paired with a well-resourced Center for Teaching Excellence AI program that provides syllabus templates, academic-integrity guidance, and explicit warnings against biased AI detection tools. On enterprise tooling, KU has standardized on Microsoft 365 Copilot (with enterprise data protection) as the default campus-wide GenAI chat, with Copilot Full License and Copilot Studio rolling out during 2025–26 and Adobe Firefly/Express filling in the multimodal stack. Research strength is concentrated on the KUMC side (Enhanced AI Center, RIMI Lab, Databricks-on-Azure Digital Research Platform) and in AAI's FLITE center, which has emerged as a national hub for AI and XR for students with disabilities, anchored by multiple multi-million-dollar federal OSEP awards. The Institute for Information Sciences' Center for Cyber-Social Dynamics provides a humanities/ethics counterweight to the technical centers, focusing on algorithmic fairness, epistemic injustice, and human-AI interaction. Kansas Professor Kathryn Conrad's 'AI Bill of Rights in Education' has national visibility and informs the university's principles. On computing infrastructure, the BigJay NSF-MRI-funded cluster and the ITTC servers give Lawrence researchers local GPU capacity; KUMC augments this with scalable Azure/Databricks. Gaps include the absence of a dedicated Chief AI Officer, no discrete AI degree program, no publicly disclosed student-success chatbot or predictive-analytics retention platform, and a notable regulatory constraint from Kansas HB 2313 on state-device AI platform access. KU's 2025 'Falling Into AI' partnership with Google and ongoing Microsoft enterprise rollout suggest the next 12–18 months will be focused on scaling deployment and faculty adoption rather than new governance structures.

gaps_acknowledged: No publicly disclosed Chief AI Officer or dedicated AI executive role at KU; AI strategy flows through the Taskforce co-chairs (identities of current co-chairs not surfaced on public page).
No dedicated AI degree/minor/major found at KU — only data-science certificates and an EECS Data Science & Engineering pathway.
No public student-success AI chatbot or predictive-analytics retention product identified; research-based FLITE tools are not deployed university-wide.
Exact NSF MRI award amount for BigJay was not extracted from public text; only award ID 2117449 is confirmed.
Microsoft Copilot deployment date and current user-count not publicly disclosed beyond 'Copilot Chat already available' and Full License 'winter 2025'.
Adoption date of the campus-wide AI guidelines (ai.ku.edu) not explicitly stated on the guidelines site.
No publicly reported AI-related lawsuit, FERPA violation, or controversy surfaced; absence of reporting, not guaranteed absence.
Kansas Board of Regents does not publish a system-wide AI policy; assumption of institutional autonomy may miss internal guidance.
